did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses deep learning, specifically Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), to perform binary skin cancer classification. It introduces and evaluates three architectures: a Modified MobileNet with Residual Blocks, an AlexNet enhanced with Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) Attention, and a custom-designed CNN with integrated Residual Connections. These models were trained on a benchmark dermoscopic dataset from the ISIC Archive to classify skin lesions as benign or malignant.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled as a 'Research Article' in the header. It presents original experimental work, introducing and evaluating three specific deep learning architectures (Modified MobileNet, Modified AlexNet, and a custom CNN) on the ISIC Archive dataset. The paper includes detailed methodology, training configurations, and original quantitative results (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score) comparing the proposed models, which characterizes it as an original technical study rather than a review.

title: New Deep Learning Approaches for Binary Skin Cancer Classification
journal: International Journal of Computational and Experimental Science and ENgineering
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of binary skin cancer classification by introducing three optimized lightweight Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) architectures: a Modified MobileNet with Residual Blocks, an AlexNet enhanced with Squeeze-and-Excitation Attention, and a custom-designed CNN with integrated Residual Connections. The models were trained and evaluated on a benchmark dermoscopic dataset from the ISIC Archive, utilizing standardized preprocessing and data augmentation techniques. The primary evaluation approach involved comparing performance metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score against state-of-the-art models like ResNet-101. The custom-designed CNN achieved the highest performance with an accuracy of 84.70%, precision of 84.56%, recall of 84.78%, and F1-score of 84.63%. The findings demonstrate that these optimized lightweight models offer a scalable and computationally efficient solution for early melanoma screening, particularly suitable for deployment in mobile health applications and resource-constrained environments.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task using the ISIC Archive dataset. The Modified CNN correctly identified 305 benign cases, while Modified MobileNet identified 297, and Modified AlexNet identified 300.

One of the two classes in the binary classification task. The paper notes that melanoma is the most lethal form of skin cancer and the focus of early screening efforts. The Modified CNN correctly identified 250 malignant cases, while Modified MobileNet identified 254, and Modified AlexNet identified 255.

Mentioned in the introduction and abstract as the most aggressive and deadliest subtype of skin cancer, motivating the need for binary classification (benign vs. malignant) and early screening. Not explicitly isolated as a separate class in the binary experiment, but serves as the primary malignant reference.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of skin lesions into benign and malignant categories
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly focuses on 'binary skin cancer classification' using three CNN architectures (Modified MobileNet, Modified AlexNet, and a custom CNN). The models predict discrete diagnostic categories (benign vs. malignant) using a final output layer with 2 neurons activated via a sigmoid function. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archive Dataset (via Kaggle)
datasets_used.description: A publicly available skin lesion dataset retrieved from Kaggle, which is part of the ISIC (International Skin Imaging Collaboration) Archive. It contains high-resolution dermoscopic images labeled into two distinct categories: benign and malignant.

use.training: Used for training the Modified MobileNet, Modified AlexNet, and custom CNN models after resizing to 224x224 pixels, normalization, and data augmentation (flips, rotations, brightness/contrast adjustments).
use.testing: Used for evaluating model performance (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC) and generating confusion matrices.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were based on descriptive metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC) and confusion matrices without statistical significance testing.

ISIC Archive Dataset (via Kaggle).notes: The Modified CNN achieved the highest performance across all metrics. The paper notes that the Modified CNN outperforms or matches state-of-the-art models such as ResNet-101. No statistical significance tests (p-values) were reported.

architecture_used.main_model: Three architectures: Modified MobileNet with Residual Blocks, Modified AlexNet with Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) Attention, and a Custom-designed CNN with integrated Residual Connections.
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained convolutional neural networks (MobileNet, AlexNet) were used as base models and fine-tuned. The custom CNN was trained from scratch or with unspecified initialization.

modifications: Residual Blocks (Residual Connections) to facilitate gradient propagation and feature reuse.
Depthwise separable convolutional layers for efficient feature extraction.
Squeeze-and-Excitation (SE) Attention blocks inserted after each convolutional layer.
SE Block components: Global average pooling (Squeeze) and adaptive reweighting via fully connected layers with sigmoid activation (Excitation).

structure: Conv3: 128 filters, output 28x28, with 1x1 convolution for dimension alignment.

training_details.loss_function: Binary classification loss (implied by sigmoid output and binary task, exact function name not specified).
training_details.normalization: Pixel normalization to scale intensity values within a common range.

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected layer followed by sigmoid activation

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Custom-designed CNN with integrated Residual Connections
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to a standardized resolution of 224 × 224 pixels
Pixel normalization to scale intensity values within a common range
Data augmentation including horizontal and vertical flips
Data augmentation including random rotations (90° increments)
Data augmentation including brightness and contrast adjustments

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to the training dataset to increase diversity and mitigate overfitting.
Applied in 90° increments to the training dataset.
Applied to the training dataset to synthetically expand the dataset and enhance generalization.

conclusions: The custom-designed CNN with integrated Residual Connections achieved the highest performance among the tested models, with an accuracy of 84.70%, precision of 84.56%, recall of 84.78%, and F1-score of 84.63%.
The proposed lightweight models outperform or match state-of-the-art models such as ResNet-101 while maintaining computational efficiency.
The integration of residual connections in the custom CNN effectively enhances feature learning and gradient flow, contributing to superior classification performance.
The study supports the feasibility of deploying these optimized lightweight CNN architectures in mobile health applications for early melanoma screening.

The proposed solution offers a scalable and computationally efficient approach suitable for resource-constrained environments and remote areas with limited access to specialized dermatological expertise.
important_remarks: The study is limited to binary classification (benign vs. malignant) and does not address multi-class differentiation of specific melanoma subtypes or other skin lesions.
The dataset was sourced from Kaggle (ISIC Archive) and is explicitly stated as not publicly available due to privacy/ethical restrictions, limiting reproducibility and independent validation.
No external validation was performed; the models were evaluated solely on a single held-out test set from the same dataset used for training.
The paper lacks statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) to support claims of performance superiority over baseline models like ResNet-101.

The reported accuracy (84.70%) is modest compared to state-of-the-art results in the literature (e.g., >90% reported in cited works like SkinFormer or multimodal models), suggesting potential limitations in feature extraction or dataset quality.
The data availability statement indicates data is available only upon request, which hinders open scientific verification.
The study claims computational efficiency suitable for mobile deployment but does not report inference time, model size (parameters/FLOPs), or energy consumption metrics to substantiate this claim.
The dataset split is not explicitly detailed in the text provided, though counts imply a specific train/test split; potential data leakage or class imbalance issues are not discussed.
The comparison with prior works (Table 2) mixes different datasets and years, making direct performance comparisons potentially unfair or misleading without standardized evaluation protocols.
